item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Immediate downvote for the first mid-sentence "[DATA EXPUNGED]" that appeared when this started becoming interesting. There are four of those, and each time, they rob my interest in and enjoyment of the article. There is also a departure from clinical/academic tone from the History onward.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a thing what kills you, in a really bad tone. I don't care if the tone is good in Russian, this is the English wiki. And the expungement is pretty bad.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, a few minor edits were made, regarding wording and tone. For example, "a stroke, which is always lethal" was changed to "an invariably lethal stroke". You're welcome.

Secondly, consider this:

Dr. █████'s preliminary conclusion: Currently, we cannot determine if the object is sapient. Maybe it is sapient and creates information that contradicts our beliefs in order to provoke us to argue and become its victims. If that's the case, killing is a way of sustaining its life and the object's behavior is the way it hunts. Maybe the object is not sapient and simply re-translates thoughts from another reality in which philosophers and thinkers came to different ideas. Further study is necessary.

Are you sure about that? It seems to conflict with this:

Currently, there is no way of finding SCP-1138's "neutral" form. In the presence of humans and/or animals the book retains its previous appearance. Handling SCP-1138 with a remote-controlled robot led to the book transforming to a tome of Isaac Asimov's letters in which he gave proof that the Three Laws of Robotics should not be used in robot creation. First, adherence to the laws will lead to those laws being broken, hence they are useless. Second, robots are more perfect than humans and should not be restrained in their actions regarding humans. The attempt was stopped; the robot and the remote controller used in the experiment were examined. Examination showed [DATA EXPUNGED].

What was the book reacting to? Was it the robot, or the human operating the robot? If it's the operator, then we don't currently know if the book is sentient, but we can probably figure it out. This is what experiment logs are for.
On the other hand, if the book was reacting to a robot reading it, then it can recognize what a robot is, but somehow failed to take into account that the robot's actions were decided by a different entity. If this is the case, then the book is sentient, although we still can't be sure if it's sapient.
If we have no idea what the target subject is, I once again reccomend controlled experiments. Why would the Foundation simply conclude "We don't know if the object is sentient." if they can easily find out? We have Class D personnel! Just expend some disposable philosophical guys and insert experiment logs! I'm pretty sure that understanding more about anomalies is important for containing them.

Execution aside, I like this. I'm interested in the idea of an anomalous devil's advocate. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Employees responsible for SCP-1138 got lucky

I could be wrong since I'm new, but this isn't clinical in tone, right? I feel saddened by this SCP; it's got a great concept regarding challenging philosophical beliefs and hurting people contradicting it, but the expungements, as others have said above, are a bit too much and can benefit from being replaced with actual wording. Still upvoted, particularly with the notes by Cicero and others, and though I understand that it came from a Russian wiki translation, I'm surprised this wasn't cleared out for revisions as much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, but the "kills you for disagreeing with it" part feels like tacked-on danger. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept and even a good part of the execution, but it practically demands cleanup in the Asimov paragraph (and could use it at some other places), and the aneurysms feel rather tacked on. If this thing were actively malicious, there's plenty of more interesting ways in which that could work without going full on "deathz magic".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this article, but I have 1 nitpick

…a tome of Isaac Asimov's letters in which he gave proof that the Three Laws of Robotics should not be used in robot creation.

From what I understand of Asimov's works (and please correct me if I'm wrong, I'm not an expert…I'm a casual fan at best), most of his stories were highlighting the flaws in The 3 Laws. One example that comes to mind was a robot that was stuck in a loop because a human told it to gather a dangerous material (the laws commanding them to obey humans but prevent harm to themselves were conflicting). Or if you're a fan of 'popular' movies, the film version of 'I, Robot' also is a good example of Asimov showing loopholes in the laws.

So in my opinion, the book would of transformed into a tome of Asimov's letters supporting the creation of The 3 laws.

Of course, I realize the book changes to the view of the reader, so perhaps the robot was not 'aware' of the meaning behind the Asimov laws.

In any case, this gets a +1 from me, I love this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the examples and the concept, but the part where it kills people is kind of eeh. Could be more interesting if it did something odd to them instead of just stroking them and puff.
Upvoted for the examples and the historical factor (first RU SCP in the english wiki)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this article. A fascinating concept with cool examples, written in damn good tone. Probably one of the best things we've ever gotten from the translation sites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone is lacking, I've read one comment from the author where he says that this scip has a perfect clinical tone in russian, that's great, but languages vary greatly from one to the other (I know, Im studying to be a spanish/english translator) So that's not really a good excuse.

This particular sentence jumps out to me

Employees responsible for SCP-1138 got lucky

That's really common speech, no scientist in the world would ever think about phrasing anything formal with such an expression.

The whole "teleports if not fed" thing is pretty tacky and overdone as of now, the scip is interesting enough as it is, there's no need to add this particular characteristic (I may also add that if the book shows this particular trait, then it should be re-categorized to "euclid" because it would fail the locked box test)

The failure to correct the multiple mistakes this scip clearly has, even after they have been pointed out multiple times, forces me to downvote it, even as the idea is interesting and original.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool. It could benefit for some cleanup on tone, but the basic idea is very intriguing, including the O5 note at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Siigh…and the SCP Nazi returns. Seriously, despite appearances, I really don't like acting like I'm an actual thing when I'm really just a nobody with OCPD.

Then don't. Also, please don't just randomly throw out whatever mental illness you have. A very large percentage of site members have some disorder or another, and we all get along just fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sigh…and the SCP Nazi returns. Seriously, despite appearances, I really don't like acting like I'm an actual thing when I'm really just a nobody with OCPD.

In this case: An SCP object is Safe if, when you lock it in a box and do nothing to, with, or about it, nothing at all will happen. Euclid means that the locked-box test might result in something happening, and Keter means that an object must be constantly, actively contained, or Bad Things will happen. Okay, my understanding of Euclid and Keter are a bit shakier than my understanding of Safe, but the point is, if you lock this particular thing in a box and do nothing to, with, or about it, it will eventually go somewhere to be found so that Anomalous Things can happen. Simply put, it fails the locked-box test. Ergo, it is not Safe.

+1 regardless - I was actually really surprised this had such a low rating, I had no problems with it until I read the discussion, and even then, it's a really cool idea. :)

Edit: I see someone already clarified that and this is currently classified as Euclid. My point still stands, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The expungment left me wanting more, and I found the article overall… uncompelling. I think it could be fixed up, starting with those redactions on the details of what happens to academics. Downvote until then, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you see, my idea was that it is a document for common reading and general access, so some special information here is censored or expunged. The idea also was that employees who's responsible for containing this object have special version of article, there all expunged data and all censored information are presented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. My main nitpick is that you censor WAY too much of the containment procedures. I'm okay with "Dr. █████" and "Sector-██" but censoring the number of references and number of other personnel is going too far, in my opinion.

Also, I think it's neat to see one of the articles from the Russian site over here :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The part about the laws is very relevant to the article, it is just misplaced. Specifically, since you are in the middle of describing an attempt to manipulate the item, explaining the exact details of Asimov's anti-law writing is not pertinant.

I'd suggest cutting "First, adherence to the laws will lead to those laws being broken, hence they are useless. Second, robots are more perfect than humans and should not be restrained in their actions regarding humans." from that section, while leaving the rest. The title alone is enough there, and saying the test was stopped finishes the discussion.

Then add the cut sentences to the addendum, I'd use the format
-From the aborted attempt at remote manipulation, Asimov's counter examples included: First, adherence to the laws will lead to those laws being broken, hence they are useless. Second, robots are more perfect than humans and should not be restrained in their actions regarding humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I reread this, I can see that the tone is quite strong. I still think that the placement of the arguments against Asimov's laws is not ideal. I suspect noticing that made some of the places where you chose different words than I would have stand out more than usual. In hindsight, only that and the preliminary conclusion have tonal issues.

The tone of an SCP article is supposed to be as concise and on topic as possible. Explaining that the book transformed into an argument against Asimov's laws makes sense, because it provides significant information about how and why the test failed. Explaining what the particular arguments were does not make sense at that point, because they don't provide any significant information relevant to how or why the test failed.

That said, I liked the arguments themselves, and don't want to see them deleted, just moved to the addendum, where you've already described other arguments the book has made, and they wouldn't seem out of place. In fact, I wouldn't mind seeing a few more details about how the book actually argued for some of the other authors' reversed viewpoints. I think that the arguments are one of this page's greatest strengths, as I for one am naturally inclined to think up counterarguments, which makes it apparent to me on a more personal scale how easily I would fall prey to this item, if I actually read it.

The problem with your use of [DATA EXPUNGED] in the robot test is that it doesn't really imply much of interest. Ideally, [DATA EXPUNGED] is used when you want to suggest something to the reader, but want the reader to imagine the details, because leaving them undefined is more frightening or intriguing, and whatever individual details they can imagine would be more frightening or interesting to them because of personal differences in fears and interests.

In this case, the data you've excluded does not suggest anything like that to me. The only unusual or frightening thing that I can think of that would make sense there was that it somehow made the robot intelligent enough to be affected by it, which does not seem like something this item could do, given the list of abilities that it has shown before that. I can think of a number of mundane reasons why it would transform for the robot, particularly that it was reacting to the researchers, robot operator, or robot designer. However, if the missing info is mundane, excluding it does nothing to help your article, so you might as well include it. Perhaps this is just a failure of imagination on my part, I would be interested in seeing what you wrote for that particular expunged section.

As for the preliminary conclusion, the ideas within it are worth presenting, it's just a matter of tone. As it is now, it seems overly speculative. We can logically conclude from the evidence here that it is either sapient or not, and that it changes through either internal or external processes, so those possibilities should be presented. We can't conclude that sapience would mean that it kills to sustain itself, or that non-sapience means it gets its text from an alternate universe. Perhaps, if sapient, it kills just because it likes killing. Perhaps, if non-sapient, it gets its text from the subconscious of the person that touches it, or from some other remotely linked sapient source. In any case, we can't really tell, so listing all the hypotheticals without providing any evidence either way, or any tests that could be used to find that evidence, is out of place in a scientific article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand what you're saying through most of this, but I agree with the critics and think the article could be made better. I think it would be worth it to improve the article, though. It's got a very strong core. Private message me and we can work on this together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally, to Adam Smascher.

The inherent danger of the object aside, this is entrusting its safeguarding to a single person, which is a terrible idea from a security standpoint.

Well, you see, as I said "psychologically strong person will take no harm from working with object". So, we should think, that Dr. █████'s is a suitable person for this task. And he do not even work with book, he only keep it. Keeping of 1138 do not affect on keeper's behavior. So, it's quite safe thing. Of course wee can consider a human's curiosity as negative factor for this type of keeping object, but that's why I speak about "psychologically strong person", who obviously can fight with his desires.

And speaking about security… You know, it is rather odd to make people, who percept themselves as dead men and actually do not care about rest of their short life? responsible for handling artifacts, which can destroy whole universe in 3,5 minutes, don't you think? Where is your security now?

This censorship feels kinda sloppy. I brought this up already, so I won't go into it at length again.

Just answered: "Thirdly, about [DATA EXPUNGED]. I have all expunged data written in russian. So it's not just empty places, where I didn't know, that to write. If it seems interesting for you, I can translate this parts of text and post them here."

I don't think this segment makes a great deal of sense. It's a remotely operated drone; it isn't capable of comprehending anything it's seeing, and the Three Laws aren't really relevant at all unless you're dealing with a strong AI.

It can be explained if necessary.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK, now I'll answer for main notes and comments.

Firstly, about Asimov's part - just look up for my previous message.

Secondly, about Machiavelli. Please, read my quotation in Gene R's message:"I'm not sure about that. I've read other books by Machiavelli and it doesn't look like they contradict the ideas postulated in "The Prince". Even if it is supposed to be satire, the book doesn't work with the writer's thoughts, it works with the reader's stereotypes. So there's a reason why there results could happen with someone whose concept about Machiavelli's ideas is false".

I can add to it, that I'm historian. Machiavelli or medieval Italy or whole history of Italy aren't in the field of my main studies. But still I read few years ago some scientific works about Renaissance's culture and also some historical sources of this period too. I read Machiavelli's works as well. And not only "The prince", but other books too. I can't say, that "the prince" looks like farce. Well, maybe you're right, but even if it is so, you should understand, that "it works with the reader's stereotypes."

Thirdly, about [DATA EXPUNGED]. I have all expunged data written in russian. So it's not just empty places, where I didn't know, that to write. If it seems interesting for you, I can translate this parts of text and post them here.

Fourthly, about language style. Anthony L Brown claim, that:

I think it still needs more work to reach the particular clinical tone of an SCP article, as there were a number of places where your choice of phrasing distracted me from your message.

I don't get it. In russian language it was totally scientific and had totally same phrasing style as almost all SCP's-articles both translated or original russian. Maybe, it isn't so in english, bur I don't know then… Our translator read, I believe, all original SCP in english and one from your site helped us in making it "more english"… So, it's odd to me, that style of article is not enough clinic for you. If you had current notes, not just general feeling, please, tell it.

Fifthly, about object class. It is not Euclid in my understanding of classes. It is completely safe if simple and clear rules are followed. Here they are: 1) do not give it to a particular type of people 2) Write an article monthly. Writing of article isn't form of feeding, psychologically strong person will take no harm from working with object. So, maybe it safe-euclid, OK, but not fully Euclid. For me.

Sixthly, about

The effect on people who agree/disagree/don't care has potential to be really interesting, but as it is it's just boring, predictable effects.

They are nor predictable, they are psychologically natural.

Seventhly, about "Preliminary Conclusions". Read. Think. If explanation will be still necessary, I'll write it.

Thank you for comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well, I will think about this.

I still do not understand, why part about robot and Asimov's laws should not be in description, but should be in addendum? I know, that it is usual procedure for Foundation to try to find neutral form of objects, which tend to change. Usually cameras or robots are used for this purpose. And, as I remember, this information of object's neutral form (or information of failure of finding such a form) is given in description. So I just did the same. Maybe, the general information of failure should be in description, but the details should be in addendum we need think about it.

Well, your second note is very good. I'll try write about O5's letters soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, but I cant upvote it. The entry about Asimov's laws is interesting, but in a discussion about attempting to handle the item it is confusing to have the arguments against enumerated. The second problem I have is the inclusion of an O5's letters, it is out of place with the rest of the writers listed. The first can be easily solved by moving it to the addendum section already created.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello. Author's here. Yes, I gave permission for posting it. I can comment all your notes ang give some explanation for parts of the text which troubled you. I will try to do it soon.

I also give my permission to correct the article's language. Please, be aware of the fact, that many word combinatios or language constructions has deep meanig (maybe it was lost in english variant and all deep things became odd or just poor. It happens…). So, please, if you would like to make significant chages post them here first, so I can comment them, decide that's wrong and try to show that was original idea, so you could find a better change.

Please, also tell here all you general or specific comments and notes, I will answer and try to justify my object as it is or, maybe, change somethig in the text.

P.S.
And please, don't pay attention to my nickname. It originate frow russian language, has long history and do not has any english meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a few things about this that stick out to me. I'll list them in order of appearance.

SCP-1138 should be stored inside a safe in Dr. █████'s office in Sector-██. Personnel with Level 2 clearance or higher may access the object for research purposes with Dr. █████'s authorization.

This book is, as related in the article, pretty clearly a cognitive hazard. It dares you to disagree, and kills you if you do. It seems absurd to me that any SCP would be kept in somebody's office, with few exceptions, but even more so one this poorly understood. The inherent danger of the object aside, this is entrusting its safeguarding to a single person, which is a terrible idea from a security standpoint.

Should they start it, [DATA EXPUNGED] followed in 24 hours by a stroke which is always lethal. The theorized cause of this [DATA EXPUNGED].

This censorship feels kinda sloppy. I brought this up already, so I won't go into it at length again.

Handling SCP-1138 with a remote-controlled robot led to the book transforming to a tome of Isaac Asimov's letters in which he gave proof that Three Laws of Robotics should not be used in robot creation.

I don't think this segment makes a great deal of sense. It's a remotely operated drone; it isn't capable of comprehending anything it's seeing, and the Three Laws aren't really relevant at all unless you're dealing with a strong AI.

Examination showed [DATA EXPUNGED].

Then the potentially interesting bit is censored.

Then there's the doctor's note. I think I PM'd you my issues with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Author's reply: "I'm not sure about that. I've read other books by Machiavelli and it doesn't look like they contradict the ideas postulated in "The Prince". Even if it is supposed to be satire, the book doesn't work with the writer's thoughts, it works with the reader's stereotypes. So there's a reason why there results could happen with someone whose concept about Machiavelli's ideas is false".
If I may add, some rulers of the past have taken that work quite literally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, he did give his permission and provided some assistance with the translation.
As for the editing - you're free to improve the wording, as long as the meaning stays the same. I'll ask him about the contents as soon as he'll be online.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but not enough to not downvote it in light of its many problems.

I am also curious about the whole "lets translate articles from russian wiki and post it here" thing. Does the original author gave his (explicit) permission to post it there, or do you just use the fact that it was posted under Creative Commons? How much editing is allowed to be made to make this good, and by whom? Does the original author gave his (explicit) permission for this editing? Is he still active and willing to edit this (possibly by proxy) based on the feedback?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept. I think the Robotics part should go into an addendum and it should be tightened up a bit, but I do like the concept. Perhaps a bit more about what other people have discovered between its covers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just throwing this out there, this is a translation from the Russian site.

Upvoted because I like the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconded. I like the idea quite a bit (I think I had a similar one at some point), but the writing is super shaky.

Also, for Edrobot: Did you edit someone else's page without their consent, or am I missing something? I don't know if you guys talked about it in chat or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1/2 for tone, -1/2 for absence of tags, +2 for concept. It needs some tightening up, but as is, I still like it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, but poorly written. I'm not going to upvote this untill it gets tightened up (for instance, the "Three Laws of Robotics" tangent is kind of pointless).

Also, I should point out that Machiavelli "The Prince" (which describes the so-called "Machiavellian" outlook) was supposed to be satirical. Then again, the book probably changes forms based on the subject thought the author's beliefs were, not what the hypothetical author actually thought, so this could probably be kept in.

EDIT: I decided to throw in a little note about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could do without the "teleports unless we feed people to it" bit.

The effect on people who agree/disagree/don't care has potential to be really interesting, but as it is it's just boring, predictable effects.

Nearly all of the EXPUNGED data feels like cop-outs and actually detract from the article quite a bit.

Overall meh, but reasons stated above push it to a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the 'Preliminary Conclusions' doesn't really add anything and comes off as paranoid and genre-savvy, neither of which I like. Still upvote, but it would be a less hesitant upvote if that was removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We have to placate this thing at least once a month or it teleports out of containment and causes people to die of strokes?

This is clearly Euclid, not Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it kinda… Eh. The core concept is somewhat interesting, that it adjusts specifically to screw with the reader's perceptions, is kind of nice. But then it kills anyone that tries to contradict it, and it jumps around if someone doesn't specifically reference it (it had to have hopped around A LOT before the Foundation got it), etc. etc….

Although the bit about Machiavelli is kind of funny. "The Prince" (the partially misquoted source of "the ends justify the means") is believed to be complete and utter farce that the ruling class of Italy failed to realize was satire and took it instead quite literally. Mostly based off of all the rest of Machiavelli's writings and letters. So, that's probably one of the few cases where the book *didn't* have to make up a bunch of stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this concept a lot. The part that made it work for me was when I reached the arguments against Asimov's laws, started thinking about exactly why they were dumb, and then realized what you were doing there. I think it still needs more work to reach the particular clinical tone of an SCP article, as there were a number of places where your choice of phrasing distracted me from your message. In particular, I think the Asimov argument should go in the addendum, rather than paraphrased in the description. I also feel like the robot triggering it because of DATA EXPUNGED is a bit of a cop out, since the missing data doesn't imply anything particularly interesting or frightening. Maybe it switched to Asimov because the engineer who built the robot was a fan.
